did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning and deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet) for skin cancer detection. It evaluates these models on the ISIC image dataset to obtain results including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, and proposes an edge-AI framework for real-time inference.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the proposal of a new edge-AI framework for skin cancer detection, the development of a mobile application (SCARD), and the evaluation of four deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet) on the ISIC dataset with reported accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics. It is published in the 2023 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA).

title: An Edge Internet of Things Framework for Machine Learning-based Skin Cancer Detection Models
journal: 2023 International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA)
brief_summary: This study proposes an edge-AI framework for real-time skin cancer detection to address latency and privacy limitations of cloud-based systems. The researchers evaluated four deep learning models—CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, and VGGNet—on the ISIC dataset, which contained approximately 3,700 binary-labeled skin lesion images. The models were optimized using the Adam optimizer and deployed on edge devices using Intel OpenVINO for acceleration. Performance was assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, with AlexNet achieving the highest accuracy of 93.64%. The work also includes an end-to-end mobile application named SCARD for detecting skin cancer and recommending specialists. The results demonstrate the potential of edge computing to provide efficient, scalable, and privacy-preserving skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Skin cancer (binary classification: benign vs. malignant)
lesion_types_studied.details: The study uses the ISIC dataset containing approximately 3,700 images labeled as either 'benign' or 'malignant'. The deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet) were trained and evaluated for binary classification of skin lesions into these two categories.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet) on the ISIC dataset for skin cancer detection. The task is explicitly defined as binary classification, distinguishing between 'benign' and 'malignant' classes. Performance is measured using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) dataset
datasets_used.description: A public image dataset collected from the ISIC repository containing skin lesion images labeled for skin cancer detection. The dataset was used in this study with a balanced distribution of classes.

use.training: 80% of the dataset (2960 images) was used for training the deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet).
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (740 images) was used for testing the trained deep learning models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper reports accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and inference times without statistical significance tests or p-values.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: AlexNet achieved the highest accuracy. Models were optimized using the Adam optimizer.

Edge Acceleration Inference Performance.notes: Results demonstrate improved load and inference times compared to non-accelerated execution.

Non-Accelerated Inference Performance (B.notes: Served as the baseline for comparison against OpenVINO edge acceleration.

architecture_used.main_model: Four deep learning models were evaluated: CNN (Convolutional Neural Network), ResNet-50, AlexNet, and VGGNet.
architecture_used.pretraining: The models were adapted from pre-trained Computer Vision (CV) models. The text states samples were normalized to be compatible with most pre-trained CV models, and the framework supports embedding pretrained DL models.

architecture_details.optimization_method: Post-quantization method; conversion to intermediate representation (.XML and .bin) loaded into an inference engine for network, memory, and kernel level optimizations.
architecture_details.deployment_hardware: Old laptops (Mac i5, Intel i7, Intel i5, Intel i3) and potential for Neural Sticks (VPU) and Raspberry Pi.
architecture_details.learning_paradigm: Federated Learning setup for distributed training across edge nodes.

training_details.class_distribution: Balanced distribution of classes (benign vs. malignant)
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (implied cross-entropy due to one-hot encoding and classification task, but not stated)

classification_or_output_head.output_format: Probability of skin cancer (binary vector format via one-hot encoding during training)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of samples to be compatible with pre-trained CV models
One-hot encoding of categorical text labels into binary vector format

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to handle the limited number of skin lesion images (approx. 3700) in the ISIC dataset. Specific geometric or probabilistic settings were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed edge-AI framework effectively addresses latency and privacy limitations associated with cloud-based skin cancer detection systems by enabling real-time processing and data security at the edge.

AlexNet achieved the highest classification accuracy (93.64%) among the evaluated deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet) on the ISIC dataset, demonstrating sufficient accuracy for skin cancer detection.
The integration of Intel OpenVINO for edge acceleration significantly reduced model load times and inference times across various hardware configurations compared to non-accelerated execution.
The framework supports federated learning, allowing edge devices to collaborate and improve deep learning models without sharing local patient data, thereby enhancing privacy and model robustness.
The end-to-end mobile application SCARD successfully integrates skin cancer detection with utility features, including connecting patients to local skin specialists and online discussion forums.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the use of old laptops as edge devices due to budget constraints and identifies future work to test the framework on single-board computers like Raspberry Pi for improved affordability and scalability.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (approximately 3,700 images) from the ISIC repository, which may affect model generalizability.
The evaluation was restricted to only four deep learning models (CNN, ResNet-50, AlexNet, VGGNet), limiting the scope of model comparison.
Hardware testing was constrained by budget, utilizing old laptops rather than dedicated single-board computers like Raspberry Pi, which limits the assessment of true edge device performance.
The framework currently processes static images stored in secondary memory, which introduces potential security risks and data leakage vulnerabilities compared to real-time streaming.
While federated learning is proposed to mitigate local bias, the authors acknowledge it cannot resolve bias embedded in the central pre-trained models.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.
The study lacks external validation on diverse, multi-center datasets, relying solely on the ISIC dataset which may have inherent population or acquisition biases.
The application SCARD is designed for mobile devices but the core inference evaluation was performed on laptops, creating a potential gap between evaluated performance and deployment environment.
No information is provided regarding the handling of class imbalance beyond stating the dataset has a 'balanced distribution'.
